quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Lorentz force"?

options.text: F = qE + qv × B; the total electromagnetic force on a charge; electric part depends on charge, magnetic part depends on charge and velocity
ε = -L(dI/dt); a changing current in a coil induces an EMF in itself; L = inductance in henries (H)
F = IL × B; force on a straight current-carrying wire of length L in a uniform field B
Light nuclei combine: ²H + ³H → ⁴He + n + 17.6 MeV; requires extreme temperatures (>10⁷ K) to overcome Coulomb barrier

quiz_questions.explanation: "Lorentz force" is defined as: F = qE + qv × B; the total electromagnetic force on a charge; electric part depends on charge, magnetic part depends on charge and velocity. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Velocity selector"?

options.text: F_b = ρ_fluid · V_displaced · g; an object floats when its weight equals the buoyant force (average density < fluid density)
Crossed E and B fields: only particles with v = E/B pass straight through; magnetic and electric forces cancel exactly
Node at closed end, antinode at open: f_n = nv/(4L), n = 1,3,5,... (odd harmonics only); e.g., clarinet, stopped pipe
ΔxΔp ≥ ℏ/2; fundamental limit on simultaneous measurement precision; not due to instrument limitations but inherent to nature

quiz_questions.explanation: "Velocity selector" is defined as: Crossed E and B fields: only particles with v = E/B pass straight through; magnetic and electric forces cancel exactly. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Work by electromagnetic forces"?

options.text: Dark lines on a continuous bright background; produced when cool gas absorbs specific wavelengths from white light
Produced by hot dense matter (solids, liquids, dense gases); all wavelengths present; intensity varies with temperature (blackbody)
P + ½ρv² + ρgy = constant; higher velocity means lower pressure; the basis for lift, venturi effects, and flow measurement
Electric force does work (changes KE); magnetic force does no work (only changes direction); F_mag ⊥ v always

quiz_questions.explanation: "Work by electromagnetic forces" is defined as: Electric force does work (changes KE); magnetic force does no work (only changes direction); F_mag ⊥ v always. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Thomson's experiment"?

options.text: β = 10 log₁₀(I/I₀) in decibels (dB); I₀ = 10⁻¹² W/m² is the threshold of hearing; each 10 dB = 10× intensity
1/λ = R_H(1/n_f² - 1/n_i²); predicts hydrogen spectral line wavelengths; R_H ≈ 1.097 × 10⁷ m⁻¹
J.J. Thomson used crossed E and B fields to measure the electron's charge-to-mass ratio e/m, discovering the electron
Converging mirror: f > 0; produces real images when object is beyond F; virtual images when object is inside F

quiz_questions.explanation: "Thomson's experiment" is defined as: J.J. Thomson used crossed E and B fields to measure the electron's charge-to-mass ratio e/m, discovering the electron. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Electromagnetic force as fundamental"?

options.text: One of four fundamental forces; governs chemistry, materials science, and all electrical/magnetic phenomena
A quantum system exists in multiple states simultaneously until measured; the wave function collapses to one state upon observation
The image from lens 1 serves as the object for lens 2; d_o2 = separation - d_i1; apply thin lens equation sequentially
τ = nIAB sin θ; n = number of turns, I = current, A = loop area, B = field strength, θ = angle between area vector and B

quiz_questions.explanation: "Electromagnetic force as fundamental" is defined as: One of four fundamental forces; governs chemistry, materials science, and all electrical/magnetic phenomena. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "F = qE + qv × B; the total electromagnetic force on a charge; electric part depends on charge, magnetic part depends on charge and velocity"?

options.text: Mass spectrometer
Resonance
Lensmaker's equation
Lorentz force

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Lorentz force".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Crossed E and B fields: only particles with v = E/B pass straight through; magnetic and electric forces cancel exactly"?

options.text: Chain reaction
Ohm's law
Velocity selector
Quantum superposition

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Velocity selector".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Electric force does work (changes KE); magnetic force does no work (only changes direction); F_mag ⊥ v always"?

options.text: Rydberg formula
Work by electromagnetic forces
Doppler effect
Total internal reflection

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Work by electromagnetic forces".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "J.J. Thomson used crossed E and B fields to measure the electron's charge-to-mass ratio e/m, discovering the electron"?

options.text: Wien's displacement law
Lensmaker's equation
Combined power of thin lenses in contact
Thomson's experiment

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Thomson's experiment".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "One of four fundamental forces; governs chemistry, materials science, and all electrical/magnetic phenomena"?

options.text: Photon absorption
Electromagnetic force as fundamental
Beats
Speed of sound

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Electromagnetic force as fundamental".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Electromagnetic Force is accurate?

options.text: Electromagnetic Force unifies electric and magnetic forces acting on charged particles.
An emission spectrum consists of bright lines at specific wavelengths produced when excited gas atoms emit photons during electron transitions downward.
The wave equation ∂²y/∂t² = v²(∂²y/∂x²) describes wave propagation in one dimension.
The Carnot efficiency η_Carnot = 1 - T_C/T_H sets the theoretical maximum efficiency.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electromagnetic Force as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Electromagnetic Force?

options.text: The full Lorentz force is F = qE + qv × B, combining electric and magnetic effects.
The Carnot efficiency η_Carnot = 1 - T_C/T_H sets the theoretical maximum efficiency.
Magnetic Properties of Materials classifies materials by their response to external magnetic fields.
Optical Instruments combine lenses and mirrors to extend human vision.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electromagnetic Force as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Electromagnetic Force?

options.text: At the molecular level, temperature is proportional to average kinetic energy: ½m⟨v²⟩ = (3/2)kT.
When E and B are perpendicular, a velocity selector filters particles: only those with v = E/B pass through undeflected (electric and magnetic forces balance).
The Carnot efficiency η_Carnot = 1 - T_C/T_H sets the theoretical maximum efficiency.
Mass and energy are interconvertible: the mass defect in nuclear reactions is converted to kinetic energy of products and radiation.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electromagnetic Force as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Electromagnetic Force?

options.text: The electromagnetic force governs the behavior of all charged matter, from electrons in atoms to current in wires.
De Broglie extended wave-particle duality: all matter has a wavelength λ = h/p.
Brewster's angle: reflected light is completely polarized when tan θ_B = n₂/n₁.
Optical Instruments combine lenses and mirrors to extend human vision.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electromagnetic Force as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Electromagnetic Force?

options.text: Radioactive decay follows N(t) = N₀e^(-λt), with half-life t₁/₂ = ln(2)/λ.
Beta-minus decay (β⁻): neutron → proton + electron + antineutrino (Z→Z+1).
The Heisenberg uncertainty principle: ΔxΔp ≥ ℏ/2—the position and momentum of a particle cannot both be precisely known simultaneously.
Electromagnetic interactions are one of the four fundamental forces of nature.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electromagnetic Force as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Electromagnetic Force?

options.text: CRT televisions and oscilloscopes: electron beams are steered by electric and magnetic fields to paint images on a phosphor screen
Nuclear weapons: a 20-kiloton bomb converts only about 1 gram of matter to energy (E = mc² = 0.001 kg × 9 × 10¹⁶ = 9 × 10¹³ J ≈ 20 kilotons of TNT)
Wireless charging (Qi standard): a changing magnetic field in the charging pad induces an EMF in the receiving coil inside the phone
Radio reception: AM radio waves (λ ≈ 200-600m) diffract around buildings and mountains, while FM (λ ≈ 3m) requires more line-of-sight; why AM reaches farther in hilly terrain

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electromagnetic Force.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Electromagnetic Force?

options.text: LED circuits: resistors are placed in series with LEDs to limit current (V_supply - V_LED)/R = I, preventing the LED from burning out
Velocity selectors in mass spectrometers: only ions with the correct speed enter the separation chamber, improving measurement accuracy
Anti-reflective coatings on lenses use thin-film destructive interference: film thickness is chosen so reflected waves cancel, maximizing transmitted light
Prescription eyeglasses: optometrists measure required correction in diopters; a -3.0 D prescription means a diverging lens with f = -0.33 m for myopia correction

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electromagnetic Force.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Electromagnetic Force applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Smoke detectors: americium-241 emits alpha particles that ionize air, creating a current; smoke disrupts the current, triggering the alarm
Quantum computing chips use superconducting circuits (transmon qubits) cooled to 15 millikelvin—colder than outer space—to maintain quantum coherence for computation
PET-CT combines metabolic imaging (PET using ¹⁸F-FDG) with anatomical imaging (CT X-rays) for precise cancer diagnosis and treatment monitoring
Charged particle therapy: proton beams for cancer treatment are steered using electromagnetic forces to precisely target tumors deep in the body

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electromagnetic Force.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Electromagnetic Force?

options.text: The Sun converts 600 million tons of hydrogen into 596 million tons of helium each second; the 4 million ton mass deficit becomes 3.8 × 10²⁶ W of radiant power via E = mc²
Neon signs: specific gases glow with characteristic colors because their unique energy level spacings produce photons at specific visible wavelengths
Electric field therapy (tumor treating fields): alternating electric fields disrupt cancer cell division—a novel application of electrostatics in medicine
CRT televisions and oscilloscopes: electron beams are steered by electric and magnetic fields to paint images on a phosphor screen

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electromagnetic Force.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Electromagnetic Force in practice?

options.text: Velocity selectors in mass spectrometers: only ions with the correct speed enter the separation chamber, improving measurement accuracy
Laser systems: multi-lens arrangements expand, collimate, and focus laser beams with precise control for applications from surgery to manufacturing
Rutherford's experiment was done with gold because it's malleable enough to make foils only a few atoms thick—thicker foils would scatter too many alpha particles
Electron diffraction microscopy: electrons with de Broglie wavelength ~0.01 nm reveal atomic-scale structures in crystals—confirming matter's wave nature

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electromagnetic Force.
